q: What is the difference between online and batch (offline) model serving, and when do you pick each?
a: Online serving answers single or small requests synchronously behind an API with tight latency SLOs (e.g. p99 < 100ms), so it optimizes for tail latency and availability. Batch serving scores large datasets on a schedule (e.g. nightly) and optimizes for throughput and cost, writing results to a table or store that the app reads later. Pick online when the input is only known at request time or freshness matters per-request; pick batch when inputs are knowable ahead of time and slight staleness is acceptable, like daily churn scores.
fs: Frames it as a latency-vs-throughput and freshness tradeoff, not just 'real-time vs not'.
fj: Says online is always better, or assumes batch means slow/legacy rather than a deliberate cost/throughput choice.

followups.q: How would you serve a feature that needs real-time input but a too-expensive model to run online?
followups.a: Precompute the heavy part offline into a lookup (e.g. batch-score user embeddings nightly) and combine it with a cheap real-time signal at request time. This hybrid keeps the per-request path light while still reacting to fresh context. Many recsys do exactly this: batch candidate scores plus an online re-rank with live features.

followups.q: What's the failure mode of caching batch predictions and reading them online?
followups.a: Predictions go stale between batch runs, so a user whose behavior changed sees yesterday's score until the next job. Worse, if an entity is new since the last batch you get a cache miss with no fallback. You need a freshness SLA, a default/online path for misses, and monitoring on cache age.

followups.q: When would batch serving be the wrong choice even if inputs are knowable ahead of time?
followups.a: When the action window is short relative to the batch cadence: fraud or fast-moving pricing can't wait for a nightly job even if you technically have the inputs. Also when the entity set is huge but only a tiny fraction is ever queried, batch-scoring everything wastes compute versus scoring on demand.

q: Why does request batching speed up GPU inference, and what is the latency cost?
a: GPUs are massively parallel, so running many requests through the same weight matrices in one forward pass amortizes the cost of loading weights from HBM, raising arithmetic intensity and throughput. The cost is latency: a request may wait in a queue for the batch to fill (or hit a max wait window), and a bigger batch takes longer per step. You tune a max batch size and a max queue delay to trade tail latency for throughput.
fs: Names the weight-load amortization / arithmetic-intensity reason and the queue-wait latency tax.
fj: Says 'batching is always faster' without acknowledging the per-request latency penalty or the wait window.

followups.q: At what point does increasing batch size stop helping throughput?
followups.a: Once the workload becomes compute-bound rather than memory-bound, you've saturated the GPU's FLOPs and bigger batches only add latency per step without more requests/sec. You also hit memory limits (activations/KV cache) that force smaller batches. The sweet spot is the knee where you're FLOPs-saturated but still under the p99 budget.

followups.q: Why is a fixed max-queue-delay risky under bursty traffic?
followups.a: Under low load a request can wait the full delay window just to be batched alone, paying latency for zero throughput gain. Under bursts the batch fills instantly and the delay never matters. A smarter scheduler flushes early when the queue is empty after a request, so light traffic isn't penalized by a window sized for peak.

followups.q: How does variable input length complicate batching the same way?
followups.a: Padding all sequences to the longest in the batch wastes compute on padding tokens, so one long outlier slows the whole batch. With static batching short requests also wait for the longest to finish. This is why length-aware bucketing or continuous batching matters: keep batched items similar in size or schedule per-token.

q: What is continuous (in-flight) batching in LLM serving and why is it better than static batching?
a: Static batching groups requests once and runs them to completion together, so short requests are stuck waiting for the longest one to finish and freed slots sit idle. Continuous batching schedules at the token level: after every decode step the scheduler can evict finished sequences and admit new ones into the running batch, keeping the GPU saturated. Engines like vLLM, TensorRT-LLM (in-flight batching), and SGLang use this to deliver multiple-x higher throughput at lower p50 latency.
fs: Says it schedules per-decode-step and admits/evicts sequences mid-flight, citing a real engine (vLLM).
fj: Confuses it with just 'a bigger batch' or 'dynamic batching that waits a few ms', missing the token-level eviction.

followups.q: How does continuous batching interact with the prefill of a newly admitted request?
followups.a: Admitting a new request injects a prefill (compute-heavy, processes the whole prompt) into a batch that's otherwise doing cheap decode steps, which can stall ongoing generations and spike inter-token latency. Engines mitigate this with chunked prefill, splitting a long prompt across steps so it interleaves with decode rather than blocking it.

followups.q: What scheduling tension does continuous batching create between throughput and per-request fairness?
followups.a: Greedily admitting requests to keep the GPU full maximizes throughput but can starve or slow in-flight long generations, hurting their tail latency. Schedulers add priorities, max-running-sequence caps, or preemption (swapping a sequence's KV cache out) to bound any single request's slowdown rather than purely chasing occupancy.

followups.q: Why can continuous batching still suffer when many requests share a long common prompt?
followups.a: Each sequence would redundantly store and attend over the same prefix KV, wasting memory and compute. The fix is prefix caching / shared KV blocks (vLLM's automatic prefix caching, SGLang's RadixAttention) so the common prefix is computed and stored once. Without it, throughput collapses under high prefix duplication despite continuous batching.

q: Walk me through the prefill vs decode phases of LLM inference. Why is decode memory-bandwidth bound?
a: Prefill processes the whole prompt in parallel in one pass, building the KV cache; it is compute-bound and uses the GPU's FLOPs well. Decode then generates one token at a time autoregressively, and each step must read the entire growing KV cache from HBM while doing very little compute, so it is memory-bandwidth bound and leaves the GPU's FLOPs underutilized. This is why single-request decode speed is capped by memory bandwidth (MBU), and batching is what restores high FLOPs utilization.
fs: Cleanly separates compute-bound prefill from bandwidth-bound decode and ties it to KV-cache reads.
fj: Treats inference as one uniform phase, or thinks decode is slow because of 'more math' rather than memory reads.

followups.q: Why do teams disaggregate prefill and decode onto separate GPU pools?
followups.a: Prefill is compute-bound and decode is bandwidth-bound, so mixing them on one pool means each interferes with the other's latency. Disaggregation (e.g. Splitwise, vLLM disaggregated serving) runs prefill on compute-optimized nodes and decode on bandwidth-optimized ones, transferring the KV cache between them, which lets you tune and scale each phase independently.

followups.q: How does this phase split change which metric you optimize: TTFT vs TPOT?
followups.a: Prefill latency drives time-to-first-token (TTFT), so prompt length and prefill efficiency dominate it. Decode drives time-per-output-token (TPOT/inter-token latency), governed by memory bandwidth and batch size. A chatbot cares about low TTFT for snappiness; a long-generation task cares about TPOT. You can't reason about 'latency' as one number.

followups.q: Why does decode being bandwidth-bound make speculative decoding attractive?
followups.a: Since decode underuses FLOPs, you have spare compute to verify multiple draft tokens per step nearly for free. Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to propose several tokens, then the big model verifies them in one parallel forward pass, accepting the prefix that matches. It trades idle FLOPs for fewer memory-bound steps, cutting latency.

q: What is the KV cache and why does it dominate GPU memory in LLM serving?
a: During decode, attention needs the keys and values of all prior tokens; the KV cache stores them so each new token reuses past work instead of recomputing the whole sequence. Its size scales with batch size x sequence length x layers x heads x head_dim x 2 (K and V) x bytes-per-element, so long contexts and many concurrent requests can consume more memory than the weights themselves. Because it grows per token and per request, KV-cache capacity is usually what limits how many concurrent requests fit on a GPU.
fs: Gives the scaling factors (seq len x layers x batch) and says KV cache, not weights, caps concurrency.
fj: Thinks the cache is an optional optimization or that model weights are the main memory consumer at serving time.

followups.q: How do GQA and MQA shrink the KV cache, and what's the tradeoff?
followups.a: Multi-query (MQA) shares one K/V head across all query heads; grouped-query (GQA) shares K/V across small groups of query heads. This cuts the `n_kv_heads` term by a large factor, shrinking the cache and raising decode throughput. The tradeoff is some quality loss versus full multi-head attention, which GQA balances by keeping a few groups rather than collapsing to one.

followups.q: What can you do when a single request's KV cache for a very long context won't fit?
followups.a: Quantize the KV cache (FP8/INT8) to halve or quarter its bytes, offload older blocks to CPU/host memory, or use attention variants that bound the cache (sliding-window, like Mistral, only keeps the last N tokens). Each trades a bit of quality or PCIe-transfer latency for fitting the context. Pure recompute is usually too slow.

followups.q: Why does KV-cache growth make throughput non-linear as context length increases?
followups.a: As sequences get longer the per-request cache grows, so fewer requests fit concurrently and the batch you can run shrinks, dropping throughput even though each token is cheap to compute. So a workload of long-context requests is memory-capacity-limited, not compute-limited, and adding context can cut concurrency faster than you'd expect.

q: How does PagedAttention reduce KV-cache memory waste?
a: Naive serving pre-allocates a contiguous KV buffer for each request sized to the max sequence length, wasting 60-80% of it via internal and external fragmentation. PagedAttention (introduced by vLLM) borrows OS virtual-memory ideas: it splits the KV cache into fixed-size blocks allocated on demand and indexed by a block table, so memory waste drops under ~4% and freed blocks are reused. This lets far more requests fit in memory at once, giving roughly 2-4x higher throughput, and blocks can even be shared across requests for common prefixes.
fs: Maps it to OS paging, cites the fragmentation it kills and prefix-sharing as a bonus.
fj: Calls it a generic 'memory optimization' without the paged/block-table mechanism or the fragmentation problem.

followups.q: How does copy-on-write enable safe KV-block sharing across requests?
followups.a: When multiple requests share a common prefix (same system prompt, or parallel samples from one prompt) they point at the same physical KV blocks via the block table. As soon as one diverges and must write new tokens, that block is copied so the others are unaffected, just like OS copy-on-write pages. This gives memory savings without correctness risk.

followups.q: What overhead does the block-table indirection introduce?
followups.a: Attention kernels must now gather KV from non-contiguous blocks via the block table instead of reading one contiguous buffer, so you need a custom paged-attention kernel and pay a small indexing/gather cost. The throughput gain from fitting far more requests dwarfs it, but it means you can't just use a stock dense attention kernel.

followups.q: How does block size choice affect the fragmentation-vs-overhead tradeoff?
followups.a: Small blocks minimize internal fragmentation (less wasted tail per sequence) but inflate block-table size and per-step scheduling/gather overhead. Large blocks cut overhead but waste more memory on partially filled final blocks. Engines pick a middle value (e.g. 16 tokens) that keeps waste low without too many blocks to manage.

q: Your model shows 100% GPU utilization but throughput is low. What metric tells the real story?
a: GPU utilization only means a kernel was running at all, not that the SMs or FLOPs were busy, so you can sit at 100% util while using ~20% of peak compute. The honest metrics are Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) for compute-heavy work and Memory Bandwidth Utilization (MBU) for memory-bound decode. Low MFU at high util usually means small batches, memory-bandwidth stalls, or poor kernel efficiency; the fix is often larger/continuous batching or better kernels, not more GPUs.
fs: Calls GPU util a misleading metric and reaches for MFU/MBU plus a concrete cause like small batch.
fj: Trusts the 100% number and concludes the GPU is maxed out, recommending buying more hardware.

followups.q: How would you actually measure MFU rather than eyeball it?
followups.a: Compute the model's theoretical FLOPs per forward (or fwd+bwd for training) times tokens/sec, then divide by the GPU's peak FLOPs for the dtype. A profiler (Nsight, torch profiler) gives kernel-level occupancy and achieved FLOPs. Realistic MFU is often 30-50% for training; well under that signals batching, kernel, or data-loading bottlenecks.

followups.q: If MBU is high but MFU is low, what does that tell you?
followups.a: You're memory-bandwidth bound, the hallmark of single-request or small-batch decode where you stream weights/KV from HBM faster than you do math. More batching raises arithmetic intensity and shifts you toward compute-bound, lifting MFU. Buying faster-compute GPUs won't help; you need either bigger batches or higher-bandwidth memory.

followups.q: Could 100% util with low throughput also be a non-GPU bottleneck?
followups.a: Yes, a busy kernel can be stalled waiting on data: slow tokenization, CPU-bound preprocessing, host-to-device copies, or a starved input pipeline keep the GPU spinning on small work. Profiling the timeline shows gaps or tiny kernels back-to-back. The fix is overlapping data loading, pinned memory, or prefetching, not more GPUs.

q: What is a feature store and what core problem does it solve?
a: A feature store is a centralized system that computes, stores, and serves features for both training and inference, typically with an offline store (warehouse/lake for training data) and a low-latency online store (e.g. Redis/DynamoDB) for serving. Its core value is computing a feature once and reusing it everywhere, which reduces duplicated pipelines, makes features discoverable via a registry, and is the primary tool for fighting train/serve skew. Examples include Feast, Tecton, and Hopsworks.
fs: Names the offline/online split, the registry, and reuse-to-kill-skew as the point.
fj: Describes it as 'just a table of features' or a cache, missing the dual offline/online stores and the consistency goal.

followups.q: When is a full feature store overkill?
followups.a: For a single team with one or two models and simple features computed in the same pipeline, a feature store's operational cost (extra infra, online store, materialization jobs) outweighs its benefits. If you don't have feature reuse across teams, real-time serving needs, or skew problems, a versioned table plus a shared transform library is enough.

followups.q: Who computes the feature value: the store or an upstream job?
followups.a: It varies. Feast-style stores are mostly a serving/registry layer that materializes features computed by your own batch/stream jobs, so you own transformation correctness. Tecton-style stores also orchestrate the computation from feature definitions. Knowing which determines where skew can creep in: if the store doesn't own transforms, shared code discipline is on you.

followups.q: What's the consistency risk between the offline and online stores?
followups.a: Materialization to the online store lags behind the offline write, so at any moment the online value can be staler than what training saw, or a backfill can update offline without re-materializing online. You need freshness monitoring on the online store and a guarantee that both are derived from the same source computation, not separate pipelines.

q: Explain train/serve skew. How does a feature store reduce it, and why doesn't it fully eliminate it?
a: Train/serve skew is when a feature is computed differently (or from different data) at training time vs serving time, so the model sees inputs in production that don't match what it learned, degrading accuracy. A feature store reduces it by serving the same definitions/transformations and materializing the same values to both offline and online stores. It doesn't fully eliminate skew because features still cross system boundaries: online aggregation windows, timezones, null handling, or freshness can diverge from the offline batch job unless transformations are genuinely shared code.
fs: Pinpoints divergence at system boundaries (windows, nulls, freshness), not just 'use a feature store'.
fj: Claims a feature store guarantees zero skew, treating it as a silver bullet.

followups.q: Give a concrete skew bug that survives a feature store.
followups.a: A `last_7d_purchase_count` computed offline over calendar days but online over a rolling 7x24h window from request time: same name, different values. Or offline imputes missing income with the training-set median while the online service defaults to 0. The store served 'the same feature' but the math diverged at the boundary.

followups.q: Why is freshness a form of skew, not just a latency issue?
followups.a: If the online store is materialized hourly but training joined values as of the exact event time, then at serving the model gets a value that's up to an hour stale relative to what it learned on. The distribution the model sees shifts purely from lag, even though the transform code is identical. Freshness mismatch is skew.

followups.q: How does logging serving features help close the remaining gap?
followups.a: You log the exact feature vector served, then periodically recompute the offline value for those entity/timestamps and diff them. Any nonzero per-feature divergence is measured skew you can alert on and trace to a specific transform or freshness issue. This turns skew from an assumption into an instrumented metric the store alone can't guarantee away.

q: What is point-in-time correctness when building training data, and what bug does it prevent?
a: Point-in-time correctness means each training row joins feature values as they existed at that example's label timestamp, using an as-of join where feature event time <= label time. It prevents label/temporal leakage where you accidentally attach feature values computed after the prediction moment. Skipping it can inflate offline AUC by 5-20% while online performance collapses, because the model leans on future information it will never have at inference.
fs: States the as-of join rule (feature ts <= label ts) and names the leakage it stops.
fj: Does a plain join on entity key, grabbing the latest feature value regardless of timestamp.

followups.q: How does a feature-arrival delay (latency) break a naive as-of join?
followups.a: A feature event may have happened before the label time but only landed in the store afterward, so an `event_ts <= label_ts` join would use a value that wasn't actually available at prediction time. To be truly point-in-time you must also respect the value's ingestion/availability timestamp, not just its event timestamp. Otherwise you reintroduce subtle leakage.

followups.q: Why is point-in-time correctness expensive to compute at scale?
followups.a: It's an as-of (temporal range) join: for every label row you find the latest feature row before its timestamp, which is a sort-merge over potentially huge feature histories rather than a cheap equality join. Engines optimize it (Spark as-of, Feast point-in-time joins), but it's still far costlier than grabbing the latest snapshot, which is why people cut the corner.

followups.q: How do you sanity-check that your training set is point-in-time correct?
followups.a: Look for a feature whose offline AUC is implausibly high and verify it's knowable before the label time. Compare offline metrics to a shadow/online run: a large gap signals leakage. You can also deliberately recompute one feature with a strict cutoff and confirm the row-level values match what the as-of join produced.

q: What is data leakage in ML and why is it so dangerous?
a: Data leakage is when information unavailable at prediction time leaks into training, so offline metrics look great but the model fails in production. It's dangerous precisely because it hides during evaluation: your cross-validation score is inflated, you ship with confidence, and the gap only shows once real traffic arrives without the leaked signal. Common forms are target leakage, temporal leakage, and preprocessing leakage.
fs: Stresses that leakage corrupts the very metric you'd use to catch it, so it's silent until prod.
fj: Defines leakage vaguely as 'overfitting' and assumes a good CV score means you're safe.

followups.q: How is leakage different from overfitting?
followups.a: Overfitting shows up as a train-vs-validation gap, so your honest validation score already warns you. Leakage inflates the validation score itself because the leaked signal is present in both folds, so it hides behind a great CV number. More data or regularization fixes overfitting; only removing the leaked information fixes leakage.

followups.q: What's a cheap first check for leakage before a full audit?
followups.a: Pull feature importances/SHAP and look for a single feature that dominates implausibly, then ask whether its value is knowable strictly before the prediction timestamp. A near-perfect metric on a genuinely hard problem is itself a red flag. These two checks catch most obvious target leaks in minutes.

followups.q: Can leakage ever be subtle enough to pass these checks?
followups.a: Yes, statistical leakage from target encoding fit across folds or a slightly overlapping aggregation window spreads signal across many features, so no single column looks suspicious and importances look normal. Catching it needs strict before-the-label computation and fitting all target-based transforms inside the CV fold, not a one-feature scan.

q: Give a concrete example of target leakage and how you'd catch it.
a: Target leakage is a feature that encodes the outcome itself, e.g. predicting loan default while including a 'collections_agency_assigned' flag that only gets set after default, or using 'days_until_cancel' to predict churn. You catch it by checking feature importances for a single suspiciously dominant feature, asking 'is this value knowable strictly before the prediction timestamp?', and auditing data lineage. A near-perfect offline metric on a hard problem is itself a red flag for leakage.
fs: Gives a post-outcome feature example and applies the 'knowable before prediction time' test.
fj: Just adds every available column to the model and trusts a 0.99 AUC as a win.

followups.q: What makes target leakage harder to spot in a warehouse where columns are mutated in place?
followups.a: If a row's `account_status` is updated to 'defaulted' after the event but you read the current value, the column silently encodes the future even though it existed before. Without an event-time history or audit log you can't tell when the value was set, so the leak is invisible. You need versioned/append-only feature history to compute as-of values.

followups.q: How do you handle a feature that's predictive but only available for some rows post-outcome?
followups.a: Drop it, or reconstruct only the portion knowable at prediction time (e.g. the value as of the cutoff). Never impute the post-outcome version. If its pre-prediction form is too sparse to matter, it's not a usable feature. The test is strictly: would this exact value exist at serving time for a live request?

followups.q: Why isn't removing the obvious leaky column always sufficient?
followups.a: Other features can be proxies for it: a downstream system might stamp a queue assignment or a fee that's also only set after the outcome and correlates with the dropped column. You have to trace lineage for every dominant feature, not just the one you named, and re-audit importances after removal to catch the next proxy.

q: Why is standard K-fold cross-validation wrong for time-series, and what do you use instead?
a: Standard K-fold shuffles rows, so future observations land in training folds while past ones are tested, leaking future information across the time boundary and inflating scores. For temporal data you use forward-chaining (rolling/expanding window) CV where each fold trains only on data strictly before the validation window. The same idea applies to grouped data: use group-aware splits so the same entity (user, patient) never appears in both train and test.
fs: Says K-fold leaks across the time boundary and reaches for rolling-window / grouped CV.
fj: Uses shuffled K-fold on time series and reports the optimistic score as final.

lines: # tests on the next time block

followups.q: What is a purge/embargo gap and why add one between train and validation folds?
followups.a: When a label spans time (e.g. a 7-day-ahead outcome) the last training rows' label windows overlap the validation period, leaking across the boundary. You purge training samples whose label window touches the validation set and add an embargo gap after it. This is standard in financial ML (de Prado's purged K-fold).

followups.q: Expanding vs sliding window for forward-chaining: when pick which?
followups.a: Expanding (growing) windows use all history, good when older data stays relevant and you want maximum signal. Sliding (fixed-length) windows drop old data, better under concept drift or regime change where stale patterns hurt. Sliding also gives more comparable fold sizes; expanding can make later folds train on far more data than early ones.

followups.q: How does grouped data combine with time-series splitting?
followups.a: You need both constraints at once: split by time so no future leaks, and group by entity so the same user/patient never spans train and test. A naive time split can still leak if an entity's rows straddle the cutoff and the model memorizes that entity. Use group-aware temporal splits, respecting both boundaries simultaneously.

q: Show a preprocessing-leakage bug and fix it.
a: Fitting a scaler or imputer on the full dataset before splitting leaks test-set statistics (mean, std, distribution) into training, so the model implicitly 'sees' the test data. The fix is to fit all transforms on the training fold only and apply them to validation/test, ideally wrapped in a Pipeline so the fit/transform boundary is enforced inside cross-validation. This keeps every fold's preprocessing honest.
fs: Spots that fitting before the split leaks distribution stats and wraps it in a Pipeline.
fj: Scales the whole dataframe once at the top of the notebook, then splits.
